literal: recognition; knowing again as the very same
invariant_definition: Recognition, in ordinary Sanskrit epistemic usage, is the cognition "this is that very one" formed through the conjunction of present awareness and prior trace. Kashmir Śaivism radicalizes that familiar structure into its central soteriological concept. Liberation is not the production of a new object nor the arrival of consciousness at something external to itself; it is the recognition that the finite knower was always Śiva. The term therefore sits on technical scaffolding supplied by ordinary theories of recognition, memory, and self-awareness. Its force in Trika lies in the claim that the self's contraction is a condition of non-recognition, not a loss of its real nature. Because of that, *pratyabhijñā* also resists overly impersonal descriptions of liberation: what is recovered is not blank witnesshood alone but the self-aware, agentive, and expressive "I" of consciousness in its unrestricted form. The term is thus more exact than the simple gloss "realization."

per_school.definition: In Advaita, pratyabhijñāna is an admissible identity-cognition by which one and the same Brahman is re-identified across scriptural passages even when the relational expression changes, so that differing upadeśas do not force a second referent (cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.27). Śaṅkara likewise says that the very same supreme puruṣa is recognized again under a later description, which shows that pratyabhijñā belongs to ascertainment of an already-given vastu rather than production of something new (cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.3.13).

Rāmānuja treats the paradigm 'so 'yaṃ devadattaḥ' as a genuine cognition of one entity qualified by past and present relations, not as a forced lakṣaṇā that strips both terms down to a featureless core (cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/25). Accordingly, recognition for Viśiṣṭādvaita proceeds through prakāra-identity, and Rāmānuja deploys that structure to explain sāmānādhikaraṇya without conceding nirviśeṣa-vāda (cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/63).

Tattva-vāda treats pratyabhijñā as evidence for the stability of entities, since Madhva explicitly appeals to what is recognized in experience to block a universal kṣaṇika reading of things (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184). He also grants pratyabhijñā pramāṇa-status in its own right, as a cognition spanning past and present and therefore irreducible to a merely present-bound perception (cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.2.82).

primary_loci: Bhāskara, Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya [no on-disk primary available here]

per_school.definition: In the Gauḍīya register represented by Jīva Gosvāmī, pratyabhijñā is not an impersonal collapse into nirviśeṣa-brahman but the bhakta's re-cognition of Bhagavān as the very paraṃ ātma-tattva now manifest before awareness (cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/line-3532). The same passage makes recognition inseparable from rati, since mere cognition is said not to suffice unless it flowers into affective relation toward that recognized Bhagavān (cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/line-3532).

per_school.not_applicable_reason: No substantive school-specific treatment of pratyabhijñā was located in the available Nimbārka corpus on disk; in the searched materials the term does not function as a technical category of the school.
No substantive school-specific treatment of pratyabhijñā was located in the available Vallabha materials on disk; in the searched corpus the term does not appear as a technical item of Śuddhādvaita.
In the available Sāṃkhya-Kārikā and Sāṃkhya-Sūtra materials on disk, pratyabhijñā does not appear as a distinct technical category or school-defining topic.

per_school.definition: Yoga does not name pratyabhijñā in the sūtra itself, but the dharmī of YS 3.14 is enduring precisely because Vyāsa appeals to vastupratyabhijñāna to show that changing dharmas still belong to one anvayin (cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14 cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.14). Recognition here functions as evidence that transformation is bhāvānyathātva rather than dravyānyathātva, since the same thing is re-identified through temporal variation (cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.14).

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats pratyabhijñā as a distinct present cognition assisted by saṃskāra, paradigmatically 'so 'yaṃ devadattaḥ,' and not as bare smṛti (cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognition-devadatta). It also uses recognition to secure a single self across different sensory episodes, as in 'yo 'ham adrākṣaṃ so 'ham idānīṃ spṛśāmi,' which would be impossible on an indriya-self theory (cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha-comm/recognition-cross-modal).

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā uses pratyabhijñā to defend the re-identification of the same word across repeated utterances, and Śabara explicitly insists that competent speakers apprehend 'sa evāyam' rather than mere similarity (cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.20). Later Mīmāṃsā extends the same recognition-structure to ritual exegesis, where karmābheda can be maintained across śākhā-variation by ananyathāsiddha-rūpa-pratyabhijñā (cite://madhava/jaimini-nyaya-mala/2.4.3-4).

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the available Nāgārjuna and Candrakīrti texts on disk, pratyabhijñā does not function as a positive technical category of the school; no substantive school-owned treatment was located.

per_school.definition: Dharmakīrti treats pratyabhijñāna critically, arguing that moment-to-moment difference blocks any genuine recognition of a numerically identical viśeṣa (cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.118). Accordingly, the judgment 'sa evāyam' is denied as a perception of strict identity and is explained instead through memory and superimposition, so pratyabhijñā cannot establish enduring unity of varṇa or object (cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.505-506).

For Utpaladeva, pratyabhijñā is the path itself: he opens the system by establishing that recognition and closes by saying that one attains siddhi through constant immersion in one's own Śiva-nature as thus recognized (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.1 cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.16-17). He therefore grounds the entire soteriological extension of the term in a prior epistemic claim, since smṛti by itself does not disclose the prior object and recognition requires an enduring self-aware subject (cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.3.1-2). Kṣemarāja condenses the doctrine by declaring free Cit to be the cause of world-manifestation and the limited cognizer to be Cit under saṃkoca, so liberation is the recovery of that already-present identity rather than the production of something absent (cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1 cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/4).

Jainism classifies pratyabhijñā among the parokṣa cognitions, alongside smṛti, tarka, anumāna, and āgama, rather than collapsing it into simple memory (cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.1-2). Hemacandra then uses pratyabhijñā polemically against kṣaṇikavāda, since recognition requires enough dravya-continuity for a previously encountered entity to be re-cognized as that very one (cite://hemacandra/pramana-mimamsa/1.2.4-8).

translator_note: 'Recognition' is better than the generic 'realization,' but even 'recognition' needs explanation. The English can sound merely psychological or mnemonic. In Trika the term marks a technical theory of liberation built on identity, memory-structure, and self-awareness. The most damaging mistranslation is to render *pratyabhijñā* as 'self-realization' in the Romantic-Theosophical sense — that English phrase imports a modern individualist self-actualization narrative, whereas Utpaladeva's *pratyabhijñā* is the technical recognition that the limited 'I' was always the unrestricted *aham* of Śiva. The recognition is structurally identical to ordinary memory-recognition (*sa eva ayam*) lifted into metaphysics; readers who treat it as ineffable mystical insight miss the careful epistemic apparatus.
